title: Audit Log & Notifications
lede: Every change in your iMash account — who made it, when, and exactly what changed — is recorded automatically in the Audit Log. Learn to read it, filter it, and turn the events you care about into instant notifications.

steps.title: Open the Audit Log
steps.content:
Think of the Audit Log as your account's flight recorder: settings edits, deleted records, billing changes, impersonated actions — it's all captured, automatically, with no setup required.

  1. In the top navigation, click User Management.
  2. In the left sidebar, click Audit.

The subtitle under the page title tells you what you're looking at:

  • Main account owners see "Every change across your account and sub-accounts" — the full picture.
  • Sub-account users see "Your account activity" — their own actions only, unless you grant them wider access (Step 6).

images.alt: Audit Log page showing the activity table, filters, and header buttons
images.caption: The Audit Log — every change in your account, newest first

images.alt: Audit Log page showing the activity table, filters, and header buttons
images.caption: The Audit Log — every change in your account, newest first

steps.title: Read the activity feed
steps.content:
Each row is one recorded event, newest first. The columns:

  • When — relative time ("9 minutes ago"). Hover to see the exact date and time.
  • Who — the person who made the change, with a colored tag: Main (the main account), Sub (Own) (a sub-account in your tenant), or Sub (a sub-account in a referred tenant, for affiliates). Rows labeled System · automated were performed by a background process — account setup, billing, or dialer activity — with no signed-in user.
  • What happened — a plain-language summary ("Deleted phone number…", "Updated lead…") plus the area it belongs to (CRM, AI Agents, Billing & Settings, Telephony, Dialers, Roles & Access, Security).
  • Importance — Routine, Notable, Warning, or Sensitive. Sensitive events are things like role changes, credential rotations, and security-relevant edits.
💡 If a change was made while an admin was impersonating another user, the Who column shows an orange via … tag naming the real person behind it. Impersonation can never hide — it's always recorded.

images.alt: Audit log rows showing When, Who, What happened, and Importance columns
images.caption: Each row: when, who, what happened, and how important it is

images.alt: Audit log rows showing When, Who, What happened, and Importance columns
images.caption: Each row: when, who, what happened, and how important it is

steps.title: Filter to find exactly what you need
steps.content:
The filter bar above the table narrows thousands of events down to the handful you're hunting for:

  • Table (e.g. assistants) — type a record type (like assistants or crm_leads) and press Enter.
  • Category — one area only: ai_config, telephony, rbac, security, billing, campaigns, crm.
  • Severity — only events of a given importance (critical, warn, notice, info).
  • Action — INSERT (created), UPDATE (edited), DELETE (removed), or ROLLUP (aggregated activity like calls and messages).
  • Sub-account — main accounts can isolate one sub-account's activity.
  • Start date → End date — a precise date-and-time window.
  • Impersonated toggle — show only actions performed while impersonating.
  • Clear — reset everything at once.

images.alt: Audit log filter bar with table, category, severity, action, sub-account, date range, and impersonated filters
images.caption: Combine filters to zero in on any event

images.alt: Audit log filter bar with table, category, severity, action, sub-account, date range, and impersonated filters
images.caption: Combine filters to zero in on any event

steps.title: Open an event to see the exact change
steps.content:
This is where the Audit Log earns its keep — field-level before-and-after detail.

  1. Click any row in the table. A detail panel slides in from the right.
  2. The colored banner at the top restates the event in plain language.
  3. Below it, the summary grid shows When, Who, Area, Importance, and Affects (the exact record touched, by name).
  4. For edits, the What changed section lists each modified field with the old value in red on the left and the new value in green on the right.
  • Passwords, API keys, and tokens are never shown — a changed secret appears as a 🔒 secret rotated badge with no values.
  • Technical details at the bottom expands the raw record IDs, useful when working with iMash support.

images.alt: Event detail drawer showing summary info and a before/after field diff
images.caption: Field-level diff: old value in red, new value in green

images.alt: Event detail drawer showing summary info and a before/after field diff
images.caption: Field-level diff: old value in red, new value in green

steps.title: See the big picture with metrics
steps.content:
Want a health check at a glance instead of row-by-row reading?

  1. Click Show metrics in the top-right of the page.
  2. Two rows of stat cards appear, always respecting your current filters:
  • Events — total events in view.
  • Critical / Warnings — how many sensitive or warning-level events occurred.
  • Impersonated actions — changes made while impersonating.
  • Created / Updated / Deleted — new records, edits, and removals.
  • Activity rollups — aggregated call, message, and import activity.

Click Hide metrics to collapse them again, and Refresh any time to pull the latest events.

images.alt: Metric cards showing totals for events, critical, warnings, impersonated actions, created, updated, deleted, and rollups
images.caption: Metrics summarize whatever your current filters select

images.alt: Metric cards showing totals for events, critical, warnings, impersonated actions, created, updated, deleted, and rollups
images.caption: Metrics summarize whatever your current filters select

steps.title: Control what sub-accounts can see (main accounts)
steps.content:
By default every sub-account sees only its own activity. As the main account you can widen that per person:

  1. Click Sub-account access in the top-right of the Audit Log.
  2. For each sub-account, pick one of three modes:
    • Own only — the default; they see just their own actions.
    • View all — they see the whole tenant's audit trail (great for a trusted manager).
    • By group — they see activity from the permission groups you select below their row.
  3. Click Save.
⚠️ Access changes apply immediately. You, the main account, always see everything — these settings only widen (never narrow) a sub-account's view.

images.alt: Sub-account audit access modal with Own only, View all, and By group options per sub-account
images.caption: Grant each sub-account exactly the visibility it needs

images.alt: Sub-account audit access modal with Own only, View all, and By group options per sub-account
images.caption: Grant each sub-account exactly the visibility it needs

steps.title: Meet the notification bell
steps.content:
Why keep checking the log when it can come to you? The bell icon in the top header is your personal alert feed, powered by the audit log and the rules you'll build in the next steps.

  1. Click the bell in the header. A red badge on it counts unread notifications.
  2. Each entry shows the event title, a summary, and how long ago it happened. Unread items are highlighted with a colored severity dot.
  3. Click an entry to toggle it read/unread. Use the small View chip to jump to the related page, or Details to open the full audit event in place.
  4. Mark all read clears the badge; Open audit log at the bottom takes you back to the full log.

The feed starts empty — entries appear as soon as activity matches one of your notification rules.

images.alt: Notification bell popover with the feed, settings gear, and Open audit log link
images.caption: The bell feed — matching events land here the moment they happen

images.alt: Notification bell popover with the feed, settings gear, and Open audit log link
images.caption: The bell feed — matching events land here the moment they happen

steps.title: Enable browser push notifications
steps.content:
In-app alerts are great — but push notifications reach you even when the iMash tab is in the background.

  1. Click the bell, then the gear icon to open Notification settings.
  2. In the Browser notifications box at the top, click Enable on this device and accept the browser's permission prompt.
  3. Once enabled you'll see a green Enabled on this device tag, plus Send test to fire a test notification and Disable to turn it off.
💡 If the box says notifications are blocked, you previously denied the browser permission — re-allow notifications for this site in your browser's site settings, then reload the page.

Push is per device: enable it on each computer where you want alerts.

images.alt: Notification settings drawer with the browser notifications box and quick presets list
images.caption: Notification settings: push enrollment on top, quick presets below

images.alt: Notification settings drawer with the browser notifications box and quick presets list
images.caption: Notification settings: push enrollment on top, quick presets below

steps.title: Subscribe with one-click quick presets
steps.content:
Quick presets are ready-made subscriptions for the things teams most want to know about. Flip a switch on and you're subscribed — no configuration needed:

  • Security & access changes — roles, permission groups, view profiles, API keys.
  • Anything done while impersonating — any change made by an admin acting as another user.
  • Credential / API-key changes — keys, WhatsApp & email connections, secrets.
  • Billing & payment changes — pricing, payment accounts, subscriptions.
  • AI agent changes — assistants, agent flows, models and tools.
  • SIP / phone-number changes — providers, numbers, and number orders.
  • Anything deleted — every record deletion, anywhere.
  • Missed incoming calls — unanswered inbound calls, perfect for follow-up.
  • Low balance — an account drops under $5 or goes negative.

Turning a preset off unsubscribes you instantly.

images.alt: Quick presets list with toggle switches, Security and access changes enabled
images.caption: Flip a preset on to subscribe — off to unsubscribe

images.alt: Quick presets list with toggle switches, Security and access changes enabled
images.caption: Flip a preset on to subscribe — off to unsubscribe

steps.title: Build a custom notification rule
steps.content:
Need something more specific? The Build a custom rule section lets you compose your own trigger from any mix of conditions:

  1. Area — limit to one area (CRM, AI Agents, Billing & Settings, Telephony, Dialers, Roles & Access, Security).
  2. Specific things (optional) — pick exact record types (searchable, grouped by area).
  3. What happens — Created, Updated, Deleted, or Activity (calls, messages…).
  4. Minimum importance — only notify at or above a severity (Routine → Sensitive).
  5. Threshold (for activity rollups) — for aggregated activity, only notify when the count reaches this number.
  6. Only while impersonating — restrict to impersonated actions.

As you pick conditions, the live preview shows "Matches N events in the last 7 days" — instant feedback on how chatty the rule will be. When it looks right, click Create rule.

💡 A rule matching hundreds of events per week will flood your bell. Aim for previews in the tens, or add a severity or table condition to narrow it.

images.alt: Custom rule builder with Area set to CRM and a live preview showing matched events
images.caption: The live preview tells you how often the rule would have fired

images.alt: Custom rule builder with Area set to CRM and a live preview showing matched events
images.caption: The live preview tells you how often the rule would have fired

steps.title: Manage your rules
steps.content:
Everything you create lives under Your custom rules at the bottom of the settings drawer:

  • Each rule shows a plain-language description of its conditions (e.g. CRM · Updated).
  • Use the toggle to pause a rule without losing it — handy during a noisy migration or bulk import.
  • Click the trash icon to delete a rule permanently.

That's the full loop: iMash records everything, you decide what deserves your attention — and the moment it happens, you know. Total accountability, zero effort.

images.alt: Your custom rules list with an enabled CRM Updated rule, toggle, and delete button
images.caption: Pause a rule with the toggle or remove it with the trash icon

images.alt: Your custom rules list with an enabled CRM Updated rule, toggle, and delete button
images.caption: Pause a rule with the toggle or remove it with the trash icon

nextSteps.title: What's Next?
nextSteps.description: You now have a complete paper trail and real-time alerts. Round out your account governance with these guides:

cards.description: Strong passwords, session control, and least-privilege access for your team.

cards.title: Team Management

cards.title: System Health
cards.description: Monitor the live status of your account's services and integrations.

support.title: Need Help?
support.content: Questions about an audit event you can't explain, or about notification rules? Email support@imash.io — include the event's Technical details (bottom of the detail panel) and we'll trace it with you.

metaDescription: Read the iMash audit log — who changed what and when, with field-level diffs — filter events, control sub-account visibility, and build notification rules with browser push alerts.
metaKeywords: audit log, activity log, change history, notifications, notification rules, browser push, impersonation tracking, security, accountability, iMash
